chunks.text: Eric Brewer is Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley with contact information including office location in 417 Sutardja Dai Hall, phone 510-642-8143, email brewer@cs.berkeley.edu, and office hours on Mondays 2:30-3:30 and Thursdays 1-2 in 623 Soda Hall. He has a personal homepage, publications list, dissertations, and research support available through links, with Damon Hinson (damonh@berkeley.edu) as a contact.

Eric Brewer earned an M.S. and Ph.D. in EECS from MIT and a B.S. in EECS from UC Berkeley. He has been recognized as a 'Global Leader for Tomorrow' by the World Economic Forum, named most influential on Internet architecture by Industry Standard, included in InfoWorld's top ten innovators, listed in Technology Review's TR100, and featured as an 'e-maverick' by Forbes. His research focuses on Internet-based systems covering technology, strategy, and government.

Dr. Brewer's research spans scalable servers, search engines, network infrastructure, sensor networks, and security. He currently emphasizes high technology for developing regions, with projects in countries like Cambodia, India, Ghana, Mexico, Sri Lanka, and Bangladesh, focusing on communications, health, education, and e-government.

In 1996, Eric Brewer co-founded Inktomi Corporation with a Berkeley graduate student based on their research prototype, leading it to the Nasdaq 100 before its acquisition by Yahoo! in March 2003. In 2000, he founded the Federal Search Foundation, a nonprofit that worked with President Clinton to create FirstGov.gov, the federal government portal launched in September 2000.

Effective July 1, 2014, Eric Brewer works 50% time with UC Berkeley and serves as a leader at Google. His research areas include Operating Systems & Networking, Power and Energy, Security, and developing regions with programming languages.

Selected publications include works on DC microgrids for emerging regions, parallel software for computational biology on clouds, JaldiMAC for networked systems, low-cost phone connectivity, multi-user design for learning in developing regions, Stasis for transactional storage, SafeDrive for safe extensions, Autolocker for synchronization, technology challenges for developing regions, networking abstractions for TinyOS, Capriccio for scalable threads, nesC language for embedded systems, and lessons from giant-scale services.

Selected honors and awards include AAAS Fellow (2021), USENIX Test of Time Award (2019), American Academy of Arts and Sciences Member (2018), ACM SIGPLAN Most Influential PLDI Paper Award (2013), ACM SIGOPS Mark Weiser Award (2009), ACM Prize in Computing (2009), ACM Fellow (2007), National Academy of Engineering Member (2007), Diane S. McEntyre Teaching Award (2005), MIT Tech Review TR100 (1999), Okawa Research Grant (1998), and Sloan Research Fellow (1997).
